message:
feat(search): cap `limit=0` ("no limit") results via RAM-proportional ceiling

A search with `--limit 0` used to mean "return every match". On a real
~500k-row user history each `SearchHit` carries the full ~80 KB p99
message content, so an unlimited query could allocate tens of
gigabytes of heap AND drive sustained multi-GB/s reads off the
Tantivy `.store` file and SQLite — a single CLI call could crush the
whole machine.

This commit preserves the "0 = no explicit limit" CLI contract but
adds a dynamic, machine-aware ceiling:

  * `NO_LIMIT_RESULT_MIN = 1_000` — floor; even tiny boxes return
    at least this many hits.
  * `NO_LIMIT_RESULT_MAX = 1_000_000` — absolute ceiling.
  * Between floor and ceiling, the cap is derived from Linux
    `/proc/meminfo` `MemAvailable`:

        bytes_budget = min(MemAvailable / 16, 16 GiB)
        hits_cap     = bytes_budget / AVG_HIT_BYTES (80 KiB)

    so a 512 GB dev VPS gets ~200k hits, a 2 GB laptop stops at
    the floor.

  * Two env overrides:
      CASS_SEARCH_NO_LIMIT_CAP=<hits>    — direct hit cap
      CASS_SEARCH_NO_LIMIT_BYTES=<bytes> — memory budget
    Both are still clamped to `[NO_LIMIT_RESULT_MIN,
    NO_LIMIT_RESULT_MAX]` on the way out so an unclamped override
    cannot re-open the hole this cap is meant to close.

AVG_HIT_BYTES is deliberately conservative (p99-ish content +
metadata string overhead) so real hits stay well under the bytes
budget the cap was computed from.

Bounding hits also bounds disk I/O per query: the per-hit hydration
loop in `fs_load_doc()` / `hydrate_tantivy_hit_contents` does ~11
`.store` field reads per hit plus up to one SQLite row fetch per
hit. Capping hits therefore caps the sustained byte rate a single
query can pull off disk, which is what was actually saturating the
machine in the wild.

Companion change in `src/lib.rs`: the `limit` clap `--help` text
for `Commands::Search` now documents the cap, the derivation
(1/16 of MemAvailable, clamped to [256 MiB, 16 GiB] of result-heap),
and the two env overrides so the CLI surface matches the new
behavior. `print_robot_docs(RobotTopic::Responsiveness, ...)`
string gains no new lines here — only the search `--help` doc is
touched.

No change to search ranking, relevance, or result ordering; this
is purely a tail cap on the materialized hit vector.
